Engineered remainder \to quadratic probe resultant product

Let P(x) = x^3 + 7x^2 + 20x + 27, and let r1, r2, r3 be the (complex) roots of P(x)=0 (counted with multiplicity).
Define
E(x) = x \cdot P(x) + x^2 + 1.
Compute
T \equiv \prod_{i=1}^3 E(r_i) (mod 1000),
and report T as an integer in {0,1,2,...,999}.

Solution.
Step 1 (Technique: engineered_remainder_reduction; Operator: substitution_using_minimal_polynomial)
For any root r of P, we have P(r)=0, so
E(r) = r \cdot P(r) + r^2 + 1 = 0 + r^2 + 1 = r^2 + 1.
Thus
\prod_{i=1}^3 E(r_i) = \prod_{i=1}^3 (r_i^2 + 1).
Let Q(x)=x^2+1. Then T_full := \prod_{i=1}^3 E(r_i) = \prod_{i=1}^3 Q(r_i).

Step 2 (Technique: resultant_swap_to_probe_roots; Operator: resultant_evaluation; Theorem: Resultant identity)
Since P is monic, the resultant identity gives
Res(P,Q) = \prod_{i=1}^3 Q(r_i) = T_full.
Also, since Q(x)=x^2+1 has leading coefficient 1 and roots i and -i,
Res(P,Q) = 1^{deg P}  \cdot  P(i) \cdot P(-i) = P(i)P(-i).
Therefore T_full = P(i)P(-i).

Step 3 (Technique: conjugate_norm_elimination; Operator: conjugation_norm)
Compute P(i):
P(i) = i^3 + 7i^2 + 20i + 27
     = (-i) + 7(-1) + 20i + 27
     = (27 - 7) + (20 - 1)i
     = 20 + 19i.
Hence P(-i) = 20 - 19i, and
T_full = (20 + 19i)(20 - 19i) = 20^2 + 19^2 = 400 + 361 = 761.

Step 4 (Technique: modular_postprocessing; Operator: modular_reduction)
T \equiv T_full (mod 1000) gives
T \equiv 761 (mod 1000).
Since 761 \in {0,1,...,999}, the required output is 761.

Final answer: 761.

Uniqueness Test

1) The polynomial E(x)=xP(x)+x^2+1 is fixed by the given P, so for each root r of P we deterministically have E(r)=r^2+1. There is no choice or branching in this reduction because it uses only the identity P(r)=0.

2) The product \prod_{i=1}^3 (r_i^2+1) is a symmetric function of the roots of P, hence is uniquely determined by the coefficients of P. Concretely, it equals Res(P,Q) with Q(x)=x^2+1; the resultant is a well-defined integer polynomial in the coefficients of P and Q, hence unique.

3) Evaluating via Q's roots (±i) produces a single integer value T_full = P(i)P(-i); conjugation makes this product uniquely real and nonnegative. Therefore T_full is uniquely 761.

4) Reducing an integer modulo 1000 produces a unique residue class in {0,...,999}. Hence T is uniquely 761.

761
